era_context: The Carolingian Empire under Charlemagne is in its final years (he would die in 814). The Abbasid Caliphate is in civil war following Harun al-Rashid's death in 809. The Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xianzong (805–820) is consolidating military power. In Scandinavia, Viking raiding activity is intensifying across the North Sea and Atlantic.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (Maya civilization in decline; limited dated inscriptions)

events.title: Abbasid civil war continues; al-Amin consolidates early position
events.description: The Abbasid civil war (fitna) that began after Harun al-Rashid's death in 809 continued in 811. Al-Amin (the elder son, caliph since 809) attempted to consolidate his position and overturn the arrangement made by Harun al-Rashid, which had designated al-Ma'mun as caliph after al-Amin's reign. Al-Amin's early attempts to strengthen his position included military campaigns and the removal of al-Ma'mun's supporters from positions of power. However, al-Ma'mun was supported by powerful Persian and Central Asian military forces, particularly Tahir ibn al-Husayn. The conflict would eventually lead to al-Amin's defeat and death in 813.
key_figures: Tahir ibn al-Husayn (military commander supporting al-Ma'mun)

sources.name: Akhbar al-Dawla al-Abbasiyya (History of the Abbasid Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Abbasid civil war is well-documented in Islamic historiography. The timeline of 809–813 is secure.

events.title: Charlemagne's military campaign against the Saxons and Frisii
events.region: Frankish Empire (Saxony, Frisia, modern Germany, Netherlands)
events.description: Charlemagne conducted military campaigns against Saxon and Frisian populations in the northern frontier of the Frankish Empire. These campaigns were part of Charlemagne's long-running Saxon Wars (772–804), which had officially ended in 804, but sporadic resistance and raiding continued. The campaigns aimed to consolidate Frankish control, enforce Christian conversion, and suppress pagan practices. By 811, Charlemagne was in his late 60s and these campaigns represented some of his final military actions before his death in 814.
key_figures: Charlemagne (King of the Franks, Emperor of the Romans)
Saxon and Frisian leaders (names not consistently recorded)

sources.name: Vita Karoli Magni (Life of Charlemagne) by Einhard

events.certainty_note: Charlemagne's military campaigns in the northern frontier are documented in Frankish sources, but specific dating to 811 (rather than 810–812) is approximate.
cross_references: Part of Saxon Wars (772–804) and post-war consolidation
Related to 814-004 (continued Viking raiding in the region)

events.title: Tang Dynasty military consolidation in Central Asia
events.description: Emperor Xianzong of Tang (r. 805–820) continued military campaigns to consolidate Tang control over Central Asian territories and Silk Road trade routes. These campaigns were part of a broader strategy to rebuild Tang military power after decades of decline following the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763). The campaigns involved coordination with allied states and military governors (jiedushi) in frontier regions. Success in these campaigns enhanced Tang prestige and economic power through control of trade.
events.certainty_note: Tang military activity in Central Asia during Xianzong's reign is documented in official histories, but specific dating to 811 (rather than 810–812) is approximate.
cross_references: Related to Silk Road trade expansion

events.title: Viking raids on Frisia and coastal Frankish territories
events.region: Frisia, North Sea coast (modern Netherlands, Germany)
events.description: Scandinavian Viking raiders attacked Frisian coastal settlements and trading posts, part of the intensifying pattern of North Sea raiding in the early 9th century. These raids targeted wealthy monasteries and merchant settlements, exploiting the vulnerability of coastal communities. The raids prompted Charlemagne's court to develop coastal defenses and naval responses. The attacks were driven by Scandinavian population pressure, political fragmentation, and the attractiveness of Christian wealth.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Scandinavian ship burials

events.certainty_note: Viking raiding in the North Sea region during this period is well-attested, but specific raids in 811 are often conflated with nearby years. The broader pattern is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The Abbasid civil war is sometimes presented as a simple binary conflict between al-Amin and al-Ma'mun; in reality, it involved multiple factions and regional powers. Charlemagne's military campaigns in 811 are sometimes conflated with earlier Saxon Wars (772–804); the specific events of 811 are difficult to isolate from surrounding years. Viking raiding in 811 is often conflated with nearby years; the specific events of 811 are difficult to isolate from surrounding years.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 811 is moderate but unevenly distributed. Frankish sources (annals) are relatively rich; Islamic sources are later compilations; Chinese sources are official histories written centuries later. Viking activity is known from Scandinavian archaeology and later Norse sagas, but contemporary documentation is sparse. The surviving sources are biased toward elite, literate, Christian, and Islamic institutions; peasant life and non-literate societies are underrepresented. The year 811 is less precisely documented than 814 (Charlemagne's death) or 813 (al-Ma'mun's victory), and many events are approximate or conflated with nearby years.

graph_edges.note: Abbasid civil war followed Harun al-Rashid's death and division of empire
Civil war continued and intensified, leading to al-Ma'mun's victory in 813
Part of Saxon Wars consolidation and post-war military campaigns
Charlemagne's final military campaigns preceded his death in 814
